stem: An HMM is used to tag a sentence: words are observed, part-of-speech tags are the hidden states. Under the HMM's two structural assumptions, which factorization of the joint probability P(O, Q) of a word sequence O = o1..oT and tag sequence Q = q1..qT is correct?

options.A: P(O,Q) = product over t of P(o_t | o_1..o_{t-1}) * P(q_t | q_1..q_{t-1}) -- each factor conditions on the full history
options.B: P(O,Q) = pi_{q1} * b_{q1}(o1) * product from t=2 to T of a_{q_{t-1} q_t} * b_{q_t}(o_t) -- transitions depend only on the previous state, emissions only on the current state
options.C: P(O,Q) = product over t of P(o_t | q_t) only -- the tags form an independent set, so transitions do not appear
options.D: P(O,Q) = pi_{q1} * product from t=2 to T of a_{q_{t-1} q_t} -- emissions are absorbed into the transition matrix

explanation: The Markov assumption gives P(q_t | q_1..q_{t-1}) = P(q_t | q_{t-1}) = a_{q_{t-1} q_t} (state evolution depends only on the previous state), and the output-independence assumption gives P(o_t | all states and obs) = P(o_t | q_t) = b_{q_t}(o_t) (an observation depends only on its own current state). Together with the initial distribution pi multiplied in once at t=1, the joint factors as pi_{q1} b_{q1}(o1) times the product of a_{q_{t-1} q_t} b_{q_t}(o_t). Option A keeps the full history (violates both assumptions), C drops transitions entirely, and D drops the emissions.

topic: Identifying transition vs emission vs initial (prior) probabilities
stem: In the ice-cream HMM the lecture states: 'On the first day, the weather is HOT with probability 0.8 and COLD with probability 0.2; if today is HOT, tomorrow is HOT with probability 0.7 and COLD with probability 0.3; on a HOT day the person eats 3 ice creams with probability 0.4.' Classify the three bolded numbers 0.8, 0.7, and 0.4 in order.

options.A: 0.8 = transition a_{HH}, 0.7 = emission b_H(3), 0.4 = initial pi_H
options.B: 0.8 = emission b_H(1), 0.7 = initial pi_H, 0.4 = transition a_{HH}
options.C: 0.8 = initial pi_H, 0.7 = transition a_{HH}, 0.4 = emission b_H(3)
options.D: 0.8 = initial pi_H, 0.7 = emission b_H(3), 0.4 = transition a_{HH}

explanation: 0.8 = P(q1 = HOT) is the initial/prior distribution pi_H (probability of STARTING in HOT on day 1). 0.7 = P(q_t = HOT | q_{t-1} = HOT) = a_{HH} is a transition probability (state-to-state). 0.4 = P(o_t = 3 | q_t = HOT) = b_H(3) is an emission/observation probability (state-to-observation). The pitfall is that pi is only the day-1 start probability, not a transition, and emissions map a state to a visible observation, not to another state.

topic: One step of filtering / forward algorithm + normalization (2-state HMM)
stem: A 2-state HMM has hidden states Rain and Sun with initial distribution pi = (Rain 0.5, Sun 0.5). On day 1 you observe 'umbrella'. The emission probabilities for observing an umbrella are b_Rain(umbrella) = 0.9 and b_Sun(umbrella) = 0.2. Using the forward initialization alpha_1(j) = pi_j * b_j(o1) and then normalizing, what is the filtered belief P(q1 | umbrella)?
explanation: Forward initialization multiplies the prior by the emission: alpha_1(Rain) = 0.5*0.9 = 0.45, alpha_1(Sun) = 0.5*0.2 = 0.10. These are JOINT values P(o1, q1), so to get the filtered posterior P(q1 | o1) you normalize by their sum 0.45 + 0.10 = 0.55: Rain = 0.45/0.55 = 0.818, Sun = 0.10/0.55 = 0.182. Option B forgets that the forward variable is a joint and must be normalized to become a belief. Option C wrongly adds instead of multiplies. Option D drops the prior pi.

topic: Transition model / one-step prediction using the transition diagram
stem: Use the 2-state weather HMM in the figure (transition probabilities labelled on the edges). Today you are certain it is Rain, so the current belief is P(today) = (Rain 1.0, Sun 0.0). With no observation tomorrow, you propagate one step forward through the transition matrix only. What is P(tomorrow = Rain)?

options.B: 0.5 -- a 2-state chain is symmetric so each state gets half
options.D: 0.7 -- sum over today's states of P(today=i) * a_{i,Rain} = 1.0*0.7 + 0.0*0.4

explanation: Prediction propagates the belief through the transition matrix: P(tomorrow = Rain) = sum_i P(today = i) * a_{i -> Rain}. Only Rain has nonzero mass today, and the Rain->Rain self-loop in the figure is 0.7, so P(tomorrow = Rain) = 1.0 * 0.7 + 0.0 * 0.4 = 0.7. Option A reads the wrong edge (Rain->Sun = 0.3, which is P(tomorrow = Sun)). Option B ignores the actual numbers. Option C wrongly multiplies the two self-loops.

figure.caption: Two-state weather HMM transition diagram. Nodes are hidden states; directed edges (including self-loops) are labelled with transition probabilities. Each state's outgoing probabilities sum to 1.

topic: Forward algorithm recursion (one numeric step, ice-cream HMM)
stem: Ice-cream HMM (trellis convention): pi = (COLD 0.2, HOT 0.8); transitions a_CC=0.6, a_CH=0.4, a_HC=0.3, a_HH=0.7; emissions b_C=(0.5,0.4,0.1) and b_H=(0.2,0.4,0.4) over observations {1,2,3}. For O = 3,1,3 the day-1 forward values are alpha_1(HOT)=0.32 and alpha_1(COLD)=0.02. Apply the recursion alpha_t(j) = [sum_i alpha_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}] * b_j(o_t) to compute alpha_2(HOT), where o2 = 1.
explanation: To reach HOT at t=2 you sum over both possible previous states weighted by the transition INTO HOT, then multiply by the emission b_HOT(o2=1)=0.2. The transitions into HOT are a_HH (from HOT) and a_CH (from COLD): [alpha_1(HOT)*a_HH + alpha_1(COLD)*a_CH]*b_H(1) = [0.32*0.7 + 0.02*0.4]*0.2 = 0.232*0.2 = 0.0464. Option B forgets the emission factor. Option C uses transitions INTO COLD (a_HC, a_CC) by mistake. Option D uses max (that is Viterbi, not forward).

stem: After running the forward initialization on day 1 of the ice-cream HMM (O starts with a 3), the unnormalized forward column is alpha_1 = <HOT 0.32, COLD 0.02>. These are joint probabilities P(o1, q1). What is the normalized filtered belief P(q1 | o1) over (HOT, COLD)?

options.A: <HOT 0.32, COLD 0.02> -- already a valid distribution, no normalization needed
options.B: <HOT 0.640, COLD 0.040> -- divide each by the larger value 0.5
options.C: <HOT 0.941, COLD 0.059> -- divide each by the column sum 0.34
options.D: <HOT 0.059, COLD 0.941> -- divide each by the column sum 0.34 then swap to match state order

explanation: A belief vector must sum to 1. The forward values are joints, so normalize by the column sum 0.32 + 0.02 = 0.34: HOT = 0.32/0.34 = 0.941, COLD = 0.02/0.34 = 0.059. Option A is wrong because 0.32 + 0.02 = 0.34, not 1. Option B divides by an arbitrary 0.5 instead of the actual sum. Option D normalizes correctly but then incorrectly swaps the two entries, assigning the large mass to the less-likely COLD state.

topic: Most-likely-state-sequence (Viterbi) vs forward likelihood -- tiny numeric case
stem: For a tiny HMM the only two possible hidden-state sequences that could have produced an observation O have joint probabilities P(O, Q_a) = 0.012 and P(O, Q_b) = 0.003. Both forward and Viterbi process this same trellis. What do the forward algorithm and the Viterbi algorithm respectively output for this O?

options.A: Forward outputs 0.012 (the max joint); Viterbi outputs 0.015 (the sum)
options.B: Forward outputs 0.015 (the sum of the joints = P(O)); Viterbi outputs 0.012 (the max joint, the best single path)
options.C: Both output 0.015, because both algorithms total over all state sequences
options.D: Both output 0.012, because both algorithms keep only the single most likely path

explanation: Forward and Viterbi share the same trellis and differ only in the recursion operator: forward uses sum, Viterbi uses max. The forward algorithm sums the joint over all state sequences to give the observation likelihood P(O) = 0.012 + 0.003 = 0.015. Viterbi takes the max to report the probability of the single most-likely sequence, 0.012 (here Q_a). The sum is always at least the max, which is why forward's output (0.015) exceeds Viterbi's (0.012). Options A swaps the two; C and D wrongly make both use the same operator.
